coredata.dc:title: Enhancing tetracycline degradation with biomass carbon quantum dot (CQDs)-modified magnetic heterojunction CeO2-Fe3O4 under visible light

coredata.dc:description: This research investigates the synergistic enhancement of semiconductor catalysts through the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within CeO2-Fe3O4 Type-II heterojunction photocatalysts. Subsequent to the successful synthesis of these modified photocatalysts, a thorough assessment of their catalytic performance is conducted. Notably, the introduction of CQDs results in a 21% increase in pollutant degradation capacity compared to unmodified heterojunctions, with a remarkable removal efficiency of 96% for the target pollutant TC. Comprehensive analyses employing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), UV–vis spectroscopy, and photocatalytic experiments provide substantial evidence for the efficacy of CQDs in enhancing light absorption properties within the CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunction. This enhancement is attributed to a notable suppression of electron-hole pair recombination, facilitating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as ·O2 - and ·OH. This, in turn, the promotion of Ce4+/Ce3+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ cycles takes place, collectively enhancing the responsiveness of the photocatalyst to visible light. The photocatalysts demonstrate enduring catalytic and magnetic stability over five cycles, as indicated by the sustained high-performance retention. Detailed analysis using TOF-LC-MS has revealed two highly efficient degradation pathways for the for the target pollutant. The findings contribute valuable insights for advancing the performance of heterojunction photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research investigates the synergistic enhancement of semiconductor catalysts through the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within CeO2-Fe3O4 Type-II heterojunction photocatalysts. Subsequent to the successful synthesis of these modified photocatalysts, a thorough assessment of their catalytic performance is conducted. Notably, the introduction of CQDs results in a 21% increase in pollutant degradation capacity compared to unmodified heterojunctions, with a remarkable removal efficiency of 96% for the target pollutant TC. Comprehensive analyses employing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), UV–vis spectroscopy, and photocatalytic experiments provide substantial evidence for the efficacy of CQDs in enhancing light absorption properties within the CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunction. This enhancement is attributed to a notable suppression of electron-hole pair recombination, facilitating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as ·O2 - and ·OH. This, in turn, the promotion of Ce4+/Ce3+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ cycles takes place, collectively enhancing the responsiveness of the photocatalyst to visible light. The photocatalysts demonstrate enduring catalytic and magnetic stability over five cycles, as indicated by the sustained high-performance retention. Detailed analysis using TOF-LC-MS has revealed two highly efficient degradation pathways for the for the target pollutant. The findings contribute valuable insights for advancing the performance of heterojunction photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a widely utilized antibiotic class employed for the treatment of bacterial infections in both human and veterinary medicine, as well as in animal husbandry and aquaculture production [1,2]. Consequently, the discharge of TCs into aquatic environments from antibiotic manufacturing facilities leads to elevated concentrations, ranging from a few micrograms per liter (μg/L) to several tens of milligrams per liter (mg/L) [3]. This release strongly contributes to the escalation of bacterial resistance and augments the toxicity levels for all organisms within aquatic ecosystems, thereby posing a significant environmental concern [4]. Current methods for treating wastewater containing TC [5], contain adsorption, membrane filtration, biodegradation [6], advanced oxidation (AOPs), AOPs contain Fenton [7], photocatalysis [8], persulfate oxidation [9], electrochemical oxidation [10], ozone oxidation [11], and synergistic treatment methods such as ozone/electrolysis [12], UV/persulfate [13], and so on. Notably, photocatalysis is characterized by its noteworthy advantages, including the ability to operate under milder reaction conditions [14], a minimized or absence of waste and sludge, and compliance with the principles of environmentally friendly technology in the context of pollutant elimination.

CeO2 is an effective photocatalyst with high storage capacity, abundant oxygen vacancies, and excellent catalytic properties resulting from the reversible Ce4+/Ce3+ redox couples and its resistance to photocorrosion [15]. While CeO2 demonstrates high responsiveness to the UV region but limited response to the visible region, it is frequently doped with noble metals or nonmetals, or coupled with other nanomaterials to enhance its performance [16]. While heterojunctions have been widely used to improve the activity of semiconductor catalysts [17], heterojunctions are contact interfaces created by mixing two semiconductors or metals with different band gaps and electrical structures [18]. The types of heterojunctions can be classified as Type-I, Type-II, p-n, S-Scheme or Z-Scheme [19]. Xiang et al. [20] successfully synthesized a magnetic photocatalyst by integrating CeO2 with Fe3O4 to eliminate congo red dye from wastewater. It has been demonstrated that the utilization of metallic dopants with CeO2 to form heterojunctions can lead to increased charge carrier generation, thereby mitigating certain inherent limitations of the original photocatalyst [21]. In essence, the photocatalyst's carrier separation capacity is effectively boosted, and hence its photocatalytic activity is enhanced. As a result, a CeO2/Fe3O4 double heterojunction photocatalyst can be developed by incorporating transition metal Fe elements as dopants in CeO2.

Li et al. [22] conducted a study on the preparation CeO2/Fe3O4/TiO2 photocatalyst using impregnation coprecipitation method to degrade AO-7dye3422 when combined with H2O2 and UV radiation. However, it was observed that the photocatalyst had limited activity under visible light and required the assistance of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for efficient degradation of the targeted pollutant. Hence, the aim of this research was to enhance the responsiveness of the CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunction to visible light by incorporating a nanomaterial capable of effectively suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been found to be crucial in reducing the interfacial resistance between two semiconductors, inhibiting the recombination of charge carriers, increasing light absorption, and facilitating the efficient utilization of the entire spectrum. Additionally, the presence of conjugated bonds in CQDs facilitates electron storage and transfer. Consequently, various types of CQDs is currently being utilized to enhance the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [23,24]. Wei et al. [25] successfully prepared carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) co-modified CeO2/Fe3O4 MFs photocatalysts for CO2 photoreduction with excellent photocatalytic activity, the findings of this investigation highlight the capacity of CNQDs to enhance the efficiency of photogenerated electron transfer, thereby augmenting the photoabsorption capabilities of composite photocatalysts and conferring upon them superior photocatalytic performance. In contrast to conventional methods that often rely on chemical agents to modify heterojunctions, our study proposes an innovative approach. We utilize maize straw, a biomass-derived substrate, as a precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. These CQDs are subsequently employed to modify a CeO2/Fe3O4 double heterojunction. Notably, our synthesis process eliminates the need for oxidizing agents and relies solely on the composite's photocatalytic properties under visible light for the degradation of the target pollutant TC. Furthermore, the incorporation of iron into the composite imparts magnetic characteristics, facilitating its facile recovery and recyclability. Consequently, our method offers a more environmentally benign and efficient approach for the photocatalytic degradation of persistent pollutants.

Herein, a hydrothermal synthesis method was employed to produce biomass-derived CQDs using cerium nitrate hexahydrate, ferrous sulfate, and corn straw as primary materials. These CQDs were subsequently co-precipitated to synthesize CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 composite photocatalysts. The photocatalytic capabilities of the synthesized materials were comprehensively evaluated, with tetracycline hydrochloride serving as the target pollutant for catalytic degradation. Various experimental parameters were investigated to understand their influence on the degradation of antibiotics and the stability of the resulting composites. Additionally, characterization techniques were employed to examine the structural properties of the photocatalysts and elucidate the underlying mechanism involved in pollutant removal.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3⋅6 H2O) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from China Aladdin Reagent Company, isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4⋅7 H2O), and tetracycline (TC) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Company, hydrochloric acid (HCl) was sourced from Guangzhou Zixing Chemical Glass Instrument Co, corn stover was purchased from Lianfeng Agricultural Products Deep Processing, and all experimental solutions were obtained with deionized water produced by the Milli-Q system, and all chemicals were of analytical grade without further purification.

An appropriate amount of corn stover was mixed with 150 mL of deionized water and calcined in a muffle furnace (200°C, 6 h). After the cooling process, the resulting mixture was carefully introduced into a vacuum extraction and filtration unit to separate the solid and liquid components by means of a 0.45 μm aqueous filter membrane. In order to obtain a purer solution of CQDs, dialysis was added and the resulting filtrate was placed in a dialysis bag (MD44, NW1000) and dialyzed in deionized water for about 48 hours. When the dialysis process has rendered the CQDs solution nearly colorless, the CQDs solution is collected and stored in a refrigerated environment at 4°C to further ensure the stability of the CQDs.

A specific volume of CQDs solution was withdrawn, and subjected to co-precipitation with ferrous sulfate and cerium nitrate at a molar ratio of Fe: Ce=1:1 (with the pH of the mixture adjusted to approximately 9). Subsequently, the co-precipitated blend was subjected to vacuum drying in a vacuum drying oven, followed by further drying at 105°C for 24 hours. Upon completion of the drying process, the resulting solid was finely ground until it could pass through a 100-mesh sieve, resulting in yielding powdered CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts. These photocatalysts were then securely stored in sealed pouches.

The crystal structure of the composites was determined by an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) Nihon Rigaku ULTIMA IV, and the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 powders were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy with a Bruker TENSOR27 infrared spectrophotometer. The morphological characteristics of the samples were determined by a Zeiss Sigma 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM), and the elemental components and valence states were measured by a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The surface charge of the composites was determined by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano Zs Zeta potential analyzer, the TC content and the characteristic absorption peaks of the composites were determined by a Shimadzu UV-250 1PC UV-Vis spectrophotometer, and the hysteresis lines of the composites were probed by a Quantum Design MPMS SQUI (VSM), USA, to determine their magnetic properties. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements of the samples were performed with Xe as monochromatic light source and a total instrumental energy resolution of 100 meV. The PL analyses was carried out by a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The electrochemical properties of the samples were studied by electrochemical workstation (CHI660C). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker E500 spectrometer, TC intermediates produced during photocatalytic degradation are determined by time-of-flight liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (TOF-LC-MS, Agilent 1260/6120).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of photocatalytic activity for TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical reactions were performed using quartz tubes containing 100 mL of a solution of target contaminant (TC), placed inside a photochemical reactor equipped with a magnetic stirrer. The TC solution had a concentration of 30 mg/L and was selected as the contaminant system. Initially, 0.1 g of the composite material was dispersed in 100 mL of the 30 mg/L TC solution. The resulting solution was then placed on a magnetic stirrer within the photochemical reactor and stirred in darkness for a duration of 30 minutes to reach adsorption equilibrium, a 300 W xenon lamp (with a wavelength range of 420–760 nm) served as the visible light source to irradiate the mixed solution. Sampling was performed at intervals of 30, 60, and 120 minutes from the initiation of photocatalytic degradation. To quantify TC content determination, 2 mL of the solution was extracted from the reaction system and passed through a 0.45 μm aqueous filter membrane.

In Fig. 1a, CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 is depicted, whereas pure CeO2 exhibits irregularly shaped cohesive grains [26]. In Fig. 1b, the image reveals the presence of Fe3O4, deposited on CeO2 in a spherical form, indicating successful synthesis. Within the composite, Fe3O4 exists in two distinct forms: one as small particles forming irregular spheres that are bound together with CeO2 [27]. As depicted in Fig. 1c, irregular flakes are uniformly dispersed across its surface, forming distinct blocks with voids. To gain further insight into the composition of the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalyst, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired and are presented in Fig. 1(d-e). It can be observed that the CQDs are uniformly dispersed in proximity to CeO2 and Fe3O4 within the photocatalyst composite. As shown in Fig. 1(f-g), the measured values for the lattice planes of CeO2, with spacings of 0.314 nm and 0.312 nm, correspond to the CeO2 {111} plane. Likewise, the lattice planes of Fe3O4, with spacings of 0.25 nm, correspond to the Fe3O4 {311} plane. Moreover, the lattice planes of CQDs, with spacings of 0.20 nm and 0.198 nm, correspond to the {100} plane [28]. Simultaneous, chemical analysis was performed using EDS on a SEM. Fig. 1h portrays the two-dimensional distribution and percentage of various components. Of notable significance are the identification of distinct chemical elements within the composite include O, Fe, and Ce. Thereby affirming the successful synthesis of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4.

X-ray diffraction analysis was utilized to examine the crystal structures of the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 composites, as illustrated in Fig. 1i at angles of 2θ=28.712 (111), 33.01 (200), 47.948 (220), and 56.729 (311) [29], indicating the successful synthesis of CeO2 (JCPDS No. 34–0394). Furthermore, peaks observed at 2θ= 29.619 (220), 42.76 (400), 57.14 (511) [30], and 62.791 (440) [31] implied that the effective preparation of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No.19–0629). The observed weak Fe3O4 crystal peaks in the CeO2-Fe3O4 composite indicate the potential influence of the absence of CQDs, which weakens the electron coupling between CeO2 and Fe3O4, consequently affecting the composite's crystal structure. The composition of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 (Fe: Ce=1: 2) is evident from this representation. The decision to select the Fe: Ce=1: 1 composition for degrading wastewater containing TC was influenced by the potential for increased Ce elements to obscure the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 crystals, and potentially impact the photocatalytic performance of the composite.

The impact of visible light exposure duration on the degradation of TC by CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 is presented in Fig. 2a. The initial 30 minutes period represents the dark reaction phase, and as the subsequent exposure to light progresses, the degradation rate gradually rises. It reaches a notable peak of 96% degradation efficiency after 120 minutes of light exposure, with no significant further increase observed thereafter. This suggests that beyond 120 minutes, the reaction system likely ceases to produce additional active species. Consequently, we have chosen 120 minutes as the optimal duration for light exposure, considering both effectiveness and economic feasibility.

The impact of the dosage of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts on the degradation of TC is illustrated in Fig. 2b. The degradation efficiency of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 on TC exhibited an enhancement with increasing dosage (0.05 g, 0.1 g, and 0.15 g), potentially due to the concurrent increase in active substances facilitated by the larger quantity of material. Consequently, the degradation capability toward TC was bolstered. However, at a dosage of 0.15 g, the degradation rate did not exhibit a notable increase compared to the 0.1 g composite material. This occurrence may be attributed to the excessive OH- generated, which reacted with the Fe2+ and Ce3+ ions present in the reaction system. Consequently, an equilibrium was reached, warranting consideration of economic factors. As a result, a dosage of 0.1 g was determined as the optimal quantity, taking into account economic applicability.

The primary objective of this study was to identify a cost-effective photocatalyst for efficient TC degradation in aqueous environments while minimizing the required amount of photocatalyst for the treatment of high concentrations of TC wastewater, and according to the query, the concentration of TC in pharmaceutical wastewater was in the range of micrograms/liter (μg/L) to dozens of milligrams/liter (mg/L) [32,33]. Furthermore, an analysis was conducted on both the wastewater discharged from a nearby pharmaceutical factory and the surrounding water sources, with the obtained data depicted in Table S1. Consequently, for the optimal degradation conditions of TC, the study selected varying initial concentrations of TC (10, 20, 30, and 40 mg/L). The findings of the investigation are depicted in Fig. 2c, the adsorption of TC by CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 occurred rapidly, with adsorption equilibrium occurring within 30 minutes. Moreover, the impact of the initial TC concentration on its degradation by CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 revealed that degradation efficiency gradually weakened as the initial TC concentration increased. For instance, a degradation rate of 89% was only achieved when the initial TC concentration was increased to 40 mg/L. Consequently, the study identified the optimal initial concentration for TC as 30 mg/L.

After investigating the optimal reaction parameters, 0.1 g of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalyst was utilized in a 100 mL TC solution with a concentration of 30 mg/L to assess the catalytic effectiveness of the synthesized material in a heterogeneous process. Subsequently, we conducted experiments to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation of TC under varying system configurations. Fig. 2d reveals that without the presence of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts, and when exposed to visible light alone, the TC degradation rate reaches a mere 22%. In the absence of visible light, the introduction of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts resulted in a degradation rate of 59% for TC. Conversely, when the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts were used in conjunction with visible light, the final degradation rate noticeably increased to an impressive 96%. The degradation rate of the composites without CQDs modified heterojunction is only 75%, while the degradation rate of TC with Fe/Ce mass ratio of 1:2 decreases by about 10% instead of its 1:1 degradation rate. The double heterojunction composites, when subjected to modification by carbon quantum dots (CQDs), exhibited enhanced efficiency in the removal of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. Additionally, XRD analysis indicates that a molar ratio of Fe/Ce of 1: 1 was selected due to an excess of Ce elements potentially affecting the composite's performance. Control group experiments further support the findings, highlighting the significant potential of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts in the purification of antibiotic-contaminated water.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation of TC by CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 was assessed under the aforementioned optimal reaction conditions across a range of different pH values (pH values of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11). As depicted in Fig. 2e, it can be observed that the degradation efficiency of TC exhibits a corresponding increase with the elevation of pH values. This effect is associated with the surface charge of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 composites. At pH=8, the composite's surface charge is neutral (0), corresponding to the pH value of its isoelectric point (IEP) [34] is 8. Whereas TC in the form of TCH3 +, TC2 ±, TCH-and TC2- [35,36], at pH < 8, the material is positively charged, so the degradation efficiency of TC is significantly inhibited, and there will be a large amount of H+ and TCH3+ competing for adsorption; at pH > 8, the adsorption resistance between the material and TC is weakened, and the alkaline conditions are favorable for the formation of hydroxyl radicals, and the degradation effect will be better, so the efficiency of TC degradation efficiency will be enhanced.

In natural aqueous environments, a diverse array of organic compounds and inorganic ions coexist, potentially exerting an influence on the photocatalytic performance of catalysts. The impact of anions and humic acids on the photocatalytic degradation of TC using optimized reaction conditions was assessed. Experimental results depicted in Fig. 3a indicate that the presence of Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ ions in the aqueous medium has minimal impact on the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts under investigation. Fig. 3b elucidates the possible existence of anions, including chloride (Cl-), bicarbonate (HCO3 -), sulfate (SO4 2-), and carbonate (CO3 2-) ions, in the actual aqueous medium, as well as their influence on the degradation of TC. The presence of HCO3 - results in a slight reduction of the TC degradation rate, potentially attributed to the inhibition of hydroxyl radical (·OH) activity. The influence of humic acid (HA) on TC degradation is depicted in Fig. 3c, where a notable decrease in the overall degradation rate is observed with increasing HA concentration. This phenomenon may be attributed to the reaction of HA with ROS generated in the reaction medium, thereby diminishing the available net ROS level for TC degradation. Additionally, the substantial size of HA molecules may impede reaction sites and hinder charge transfer in the photocatalyst, resulting in a significant decrease in the degradation efficiency of target pollutants. Consequently, primary and secondary treatment methods are imperative to enable effective photocatalysis and mitigate the interference caused by external factors present in different water sources.

To assess the versatility of the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 + visible light system, we conducted experiments to explore its efficacy in degrading wastewater containing various antibiotics, namely ciprofloxacin (CIP), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTH), and levofloxacin (LEVX) under visible light irradiation. The obtained results, as depicted in Fig. 3d, indicated that the degradation rates of CIP and OTH reached 89% and 92% within 120 min, while the degradation rate of LEVX reached 82%. Hence, it can be concluded that the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 + visible light system exhibits broad-spectrum effectiveness in degrading antibiotic-contaminated wastewater.

To ascertain the potential efficacy of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalytic composites, it is imperative to assess their reproducibility and versatility. Hence, we executed a series of five successive experiments utilizing the predetermined optimal parameters for degradation. Following each round of TC photocatalytic degradation, the catalyst was retrieved through centrifugal separation, thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol, and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at a temperature of 55 °C to prepare for subsequent experimental rounds. The evaluation outcomes are presented in Fig. 4a. Remarkably, even after the completion of five continuous cycles (similar to the number of cycles undertaken in comparable studies outlined in Table S2), the degradation rate of TC remained consistently above 85%, with only a marginal reduction in degradation performance.

Moreover, the XRD pattern in Fig. 4b and the XPS pattern in Fig. 3 both indicate that after undergoing five cycles of photocatalytic TC degradation, the composition of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalytic composites, including CeO2, Fe3O4, and Fe3O4, remained unchanged. The characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 exhibited minimal alterations, indicating the relative stability of its crystal structure. Moreover, the XPS patterns of C1s, O1s, Fe 2p, and Ce 3d before and after the reaction demonstrated close resemblance, further confirming the recyclability and stability of this photocatalytic material. In Fig. 4c, a broad peak at 3453 cm−1, along with characteristic absorption peaks at 1635 cm−1, can be attributed to O-H bonds [37,38]. Additionally, the absorption peaks observed at 1379 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of -CH3 groups, while those at 1485 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of -CH2 groups. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 1502 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibration of C-C stretching vibration [Handbook of Infrared Spectroscopy]. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 626 cm−1 and 450–600 cm−1 correspond to Fe-O [39] and Ce-O [40] bonds, respectively, reflecting the formation of metal-oxide groups resulting from the bonding between Fe(II/III) and Ce(III/IV) atoms and CQDs. The coexistence of different peaks also indicates strong electron interactions. The weakening of both CO and Fe-O and the shifting of both Ce-O and O-H in the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalytic composites after the reaction represent the involvement of these functional groups in the degradation of TC [41].

Fig. 4d depicts the magnetic hysteresis loops of the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 and CQDs/Fe3O4 catalysts, and both hysteresis loops pass through the zero point [42], indicating that the resulting composites exhibit superparamagnetic characteristics. The saturation magnetization intensity of the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 and CQDs/Fe3O4 composites, prepared using a co-precipitation method combined with a hydrothermal method, is measured at 14.1 emu/g and 20.7 emu/g, respectively, according to the magnetization curves obtained by VSM at 25°C. The obtained results demonstrate that the inclusion of CeO2 nanomaterials greatly affects the magnetic properties of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. This is because CeO2 is not magnetic, which causes a reduction in the magnetic properties of their composites. Despite the anticipated lower saturation magnetization of CeO2-doped photocatalytic composites compared to CQDs/Fe3O4 composites, they remain capable of facile separation from aqueous solutions [43,44]. It is essential to note that the complex composition of wastewater may influence the reusability of photocatalysts in large-scale applications. Furthermore, a more comprehensive and thorough investigation of the practical application of photocatalysts is warranted, necessitating the involvement of a broader cohort of researchers.

The XPS spectral width scan of the sample ranges from 0 to 1350 eV, as shown in Fig. 5a, indicating that the dominant elements of the photocatalyst are C, O, Fe and Ce. Ce 3d spectrum is shown in Fig. 5b, with 8 peaks corresponding to four biphasic contrasts, u (900.3–900.4 eV), u' (906.9–907.1 eV), u′'' (916.2–916.3 eV) corresponding to Ce4+ 3d3/ 2, and v (882.0–882.2 eV), v'' (888.2–888.3 eV), v''' (897.8 −898.0 eV) corresponding to Ce4+ 3d5/2, and u′ (902.4–902.5 eV) and v'' (883.4–884.6 eV) corresponding to Ce3+ 3d3/2 and Ce3+ 3d5/ 2 [45], which can be deduced from the graph that Ce4+ dominates the material prior to the reaction, with Ce3+ being present in minor quantities. After the reaction, the peak of Ce3+ diminishes, suggesting the oxidation of some Ce3+ to Ce4+. Fig. 5c reveals that the characteristic peaks are located at 710.94 eV for Fe 2p3/2 and at 724.68 eV for Fe 2p1/2, indicating the presence of Fe3O4, with the atomic ratio of Fe2+ to Fe3+ being approximately 1:2 [46]. The peak observed at 717.9 eV corresponds to the satellite peak (satellite) of Fe2O3. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 5d and Fig. 5e, the characteristic peaks at 284.5 eV can be attributed to C-C bonds [47], those at 286.4 eV to C-O bonds, and the peak at 288.5 eV to hydroxyl or carbonate groups. Additionally, the characteristic peak at 528.73 eV represents lattice oxygen, while the peak at 532.13 eV corresponds to hydroxyl oxygen (OOH) [48,49]. These findings are consistent with the infrared analysis and together provide further evidence for the successful synthesis of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4.

The optical characteristics of the photocatalytic composites CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 were examined through UV–vis spectroscopy mapping, as depicted in Fig. 6a. The incorporation of CQDs into the CeO2-Fe3O4 dual heterojunction exhibited a pronounced enhancement in light absorption within the visible to near-infrared spectrum. Additionally, a visible shift towards longer wavelengths was observed, indicating a transformation from an absorption peak at 442 nm to 478 nm. This spectral alteration suggests that the integration of CQDs effectively broadens the photoresponsive range, thereby augmenting the photocatalytic efficacy of the composite under visible light irradiation [50]. These results underscore the considerable potential of this composite system for the photocatalytic remediation of antibiotic-contaminated wastewater using visible light.

The investigation of charge transfer in photocatalysts was conducted using photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CeO2, CeO2-Fe3O4, and CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4. A weaker PL intensity was indicative of faster charge transfer and a lower charge recombination rate [51]. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the strongest PL intensity was observed in CeO2 samples, gradually decreasing with the introduction of CQDs and Fe3O4, with CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 displaying the weakest PL intensity. This observation suggests that the doping of CQDs in heterojunctions substantially reduces recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and facilitates charge transfer. This finding also serves to validate the successful synthesis of CQDs on the photocatalysts and enhances the photocatalytic performance of CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunctions.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted at open-circuit voltage to investigate the charge transfer resistance of CeO2, Fe3O4, and CeO2-Fe3O4, photocatalysts with smaller arc radii exhibited higher charge transfer cefficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunction displayed a smaller arc radius in comparison with CeO2 and Fe3O4, while the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalyst demonstrated the smallest arc radius. This observation suggests a decrease in resistance at the heterojunction-electrolyte interface and a corresponding increase in charge transfer efficiency, which effectively mitigates electron-hole recombination [52]. Moreover, the incorporation of CQDs into the heterojunctions can significantly enhance the charge transfer efficiency, thus strengthening the photocatalytic response under visible light. Consequently, the aforementioned augmentation results in a noteworthy amplification in the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst and offers supplementary substantiation for the prosperous synthesis of CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts.

The valence band (VB) spectra of CeO2 and Fe3O4 were characterized using UPS, with the results depicted in Fig. 6d. Meanwhile the conduction band (CB) potentials of Fe3O4 and CeO2 are 1.65 eV and 2.52 eV. Therefore, based on the calculated band gap results, the corresponding band structures are shown in Fig. 6e. With this, it can be concluded that the matching band structure between CeO2 and Fe3O4 is interleaved, possibly Type-II or Z-Scheme heterojunction type [53]. In contrast to the individual semiconductors (CeO2 and Fe3O4) and the CeO2-Fe3O4 heterojunction, the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 composite exhibited an elevated electronic energy state. This finding substantiates the notion that the incorporation of CQDs imparts superior photocatalytic activity to the heterojunction.

Fig. 6f illustrates the transient photocurrent responses of CeO2 alone, the double heterojunction CeO2-Fe3O4, and the double heterojunction photocatalysts modified with CQDs. It can be concluded that the single catalysts all have correspondingly weaker photocurrent responses compared to their composite photocatalysts, which is caused by the rapid recombination of e-/h+ pairs. That is, they also mutually corroborate the successful composition of their heterojunctions, which can effectively suppress the rapid binding of electrons and holes. The heterojunction was modified by CQDs, exhibits the most robust photocurrent response. Furthermore, this observation serves as further confirmation of the existence of CQDs within the photocatalysts and their capacity to effectively enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of heterojunctions.

P-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were employed as trapping agents for ·O2 - and ·OH radicals, respectively [54], Ammonium oxalate (AO) was used as a trapping agent for h+ [55]. It can be concluded from Fig. 7a that isopropanol and p-benzoquinone have a very obvious inhibitory effect on their photocatalytic degradation, and their degradation effects decreased by about 47% and 43%, respectively, while the effect on their photocatalytic degradation process was not obvious after the addition of excess ammonium oxalate, and even showed a slight promotion of the degradation of TC, probably because AO can inhibit photoinduced carrier recombination and can generate more photoinduced electrons, and this could accelerate the and facilitating the cycling between Fe2+/Fe3+ and Ce3+/Ce4+, which enhances the degradation of TC [56].

Furthermore, the outcomes presented in Fig. 7b and Fig. 7c indicate that ·O2 - and ·OH radicals play crucial roles in the photocatalytic degradation of TC in the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalytic composites. The presence of ·O2 - and ·OH radicals was further quantified using the EPR method, with 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) employed as a trapping agent.

The degradation mechanism of tetracycline was elucidated by identifying degradation intermediates through TOF-LC-MS analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 8 (The results presented in Supplementary Fig. S1-S3 highlight the presence of twelve distinct intermediates observed throughout the degradation process). The tetracycline molecule (m/z=445) initially underwent decomposition into two primary products, P1 (m/z=429) and P2 (m/z=415), attributed to free radical attacks on specific atoms. In pathway 1, P1 underwent further dehydroxylation and demethylation processes, leading to the formation of P3 (m/z=300). Subsequent oxidative decomposition of the terminal group by reactive oxygen species (ROS) resulted in the generation of P5 (m/z=226) through ring opening and dehydroxylation reactions. Additionally, oxidation, dehydration, and dehydrogenation of the benzene ring led to the formation of P7 (m/z=157). In pathway 2, P2 was subjected to demethylation and deamination processes, producing P4, which was subsequently transformed into P6 (m/z=164) through consecutive demethylation, ring opening, and dehydroxylation reactions. Following ROS induced reactions, including ring opening, dehydration, and dehydrogenation, P6 underwent conversion into P8 (m/z=121). Ultimately, P7 and P8 underwent further decomposition into smaller molecules such as P9 (m/z=90), P10 (m/z=73), P11 (m/z=61), and P12 (m/z=59), eventually progressing towards mineralization into carbon dioxide and water.

Based on the aforementioned findings, we propose a photocatalytic mechanism for the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation by CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts, as depicted in Fig. 9. The intrinsic energy band alignment of CeO2-Fe3O4 enables the formation of a Type-II heterojunction [57]. With the introduction of CQDs, they assume the role of charge carrier mediators, facilitating the transport and separation of electron-hole pairs between CeO2 and Fe3O4. When the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts are illuminated with light, valence band holes (EVB=1.927 eV/NHE) are generated on the surface of CeO2 and migrate to the valence band of Fe3O4 (EVB=1.012 eV/NHE). Simultaneously, the photo-induced electrons move in the opposite direction towards CeO2, aided by the CQDs' capability to promote efficient electron and hole transport within the heterojunction. As a result, the photo-induced electrons and holes are effectively separated and accumulated within CeO2 and Fe3O4, respectively, leading to enhanced carrier separation efficiency and prolonged carrier lifetimes. Subsequent to this, the remaining valence band holes in Fe3O4 engage with either water or hydroxyl ions, generating hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Simultaneously, the accumulated electrons in the conduction band of CeO2 interact with dissolved oxygen in water, leading to the production of superoxide radicals (·O2 -). Notably, these reactive species significantly contribute to the degradation of TC. Furthermore, the presence of CQDs facilitates a synergistic catalytic effect between Fe3+/Fe2+ and Ce4+/Ce3+, thereby promoting electron transfer from oxygen vacancies. Ultimately, the combined influence of these active species effectively expedite the degradation of TC, and the corresponding chemical reactions can be expressed as follows:

The inclusion of CQDs as electron transport pathways has demonstrated significant enhancement of the photosensitivity of Type-II double heterojunction CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalysts. This modification has resulted in a broadened absorption of visible light by the CQDs@CeO2-Fe3O4 photocatalyst and a remarkable suppression of the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Under visible light irradiation, the photocatalyst initiated the production of ROS (·O2 -, ·OH) and facilitated electron transfer between the diverse valence states of cerium and iron elements, while the CQDs catalyzed the generation of reactive oxides and the interconversion between Fe3+/Fe2+ and Ce4+/Ce3+. This synergistic mechanism efficiently eliminated target contaminants (TCs) from polluted aqueous solutions. By optimizing the photocatalyst dosage and light conditions, an impressive 96.8% degradation of TCs was achieved, with neutral or alkaline conditions proving more effective than acidic conditions, which exhibited a greater inhibitory effect on TC degradation. It is noteworthy that the photocatalyst maintained a robust stability, with a degradation rate of 90% even after five experimental repetitions. Additionally, a plausible degradation pathway of TC was proposed based on the detection of 12 identified intermediates using TOF-LC-MS. Therefore, this study furnish a promising methodology for enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of TC by leveraging CQDs to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs in CeO2-Fe3O4.
